coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent probe based nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots with solid-state fluorescence for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous solution
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jiantao
Chu, Huiyuan
Wang, Tianshu
Wang, Chaozhan
Wei, Yinmao
coredata.dc:description: Novel nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) have been synthesized through a solvothermal method with tartaric acid and L-arginine as the precursors. The NCQDs appear blue fluorescence in aqueous solution and yellow-green fluorescence in solid state and the solution of NCQDs exhibits the quantum yield of 8.3%, good photostability, resistance to acid or base. It was found that the NCQDs have potential as ink in information encryption and a fluorescence probe in the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+. Under optimal conditions, response is linear (R2 ≥ 0.99) in the range of 0–5.0 μM (Hg2+) and 0–70 μM (Fe3+), the limits of detection are 0.017 μM (Hg2+) and 0.50 μM (Fe3+) (S/N = 3), respectively. The established method was successfully applied to the determination of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in tap and river water samples. The results indicate the NCQDs-based probe has potential in real sample analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
L-arginine
Fluorescence probe
Hg2+ and Fe3+ detection
Solid fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Fluorescence assay of Hg2+ and Fe3+
Detecting of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in real samples
Synthesis and chemical constitution of NCQDs
Stability of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
Mechanism of response to Hg2+ and Fe3+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775121 and 21974106 ) and the Scientific Research Program Funded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (No. 17JS134 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XPS full survey spectra of NCQDs. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (b), O1s (c), and N1s (d).

(a) Fluorescence excitation (black line) and emission (red line) spectra of NCQDs. Inset represents the photographs of NCQDs under visible (left) and UV (right, 365 nm) lights. (b) Emission spectra of NCQDs at different excitation wavelength from 350 nm to 400 nm. (c) Normalized fluorescence emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths. (d) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of NCQDs.
Fluorescence spectra of solutions of NCQDs containing 200 μM of various metal ions in phosphate buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.0).

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCQDs containing different concentrations of Hg2+. (b) Plot of fluorescence intensity against Hg2+ concentrations. Inset is the linear response curve for the detection of Hg2+. (c) Fluorescence emission spectra of NCQDs containing different concentrations of Fe3+. (d) Plot of fluorescence intensity against Fe3+ concentration. Inset is the linear response curve for the detection of Fe3+.
Comparison of the method in this work with the reported methods.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Phosphorus and chlorine co-doped carbon dots.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: f
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Algal derived nitrogen phosphorous carbon dots.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The determination of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in the mixture of two metal ions in tap water (n = 3).

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Sample 1: 1 μM Hg2+ and 10 μM Fe3+ in the solution.
Sample 2: 2 μM Hg2+ and 20 μM Fe3+ in the solution.
Sample 3: 3 μM Hg2+ and 30 μM Fe3+ in the solution.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent probe based nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots with solid-state fluorescence for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous solution

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710127, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life Science, Northwest University, Xi’an 710069, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) have been synthesized through a solvothermal method with tartaric acid and L-arginine as the precursors. The NCQDs appear blue fluorescence in aqueous solution and yellow-green fluorescence in solid state and the solution of NCQDs exhibits the quantum yield of 8.3%, good photostability, resistance to acid or base. It was found that the NCQDs have potential as ink in information encryption and a fluorescence probe in the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+. Under optimal conditions, response is linear (R2 ≥ 0.99) in the range of 0–5.0 μM (Hg2+) and 0–70 μM (Fe3+), the limits of detection are 0.017 μM (Hg2+) and 0.50 μM (Fe3+) (S/N = 3), respectively. The established method was successfully applied to the determination of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in tap and river water samples. The results indicate the NCQDs-based probe has potential in real sample analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ions possess different effects in vivo. Some heavy metal ions are toxic. As one of the most toxic ions, low dose of mercury exposure may led to digestive, kidney and especially neurological diseases [1,2]. Whereas some heavy metal ions are indispensable elements for biological growth. For instance, iron ions, being an important part of hemoglobin, involve in many metabolic processes [3]. However, excessive or insufficient iron in the body can cause biological disorders and diseases, such as iron deficiency anemia, liver injury, renal failure, heart disease and cancer [4]. Thus, it is essential to detect Hg2+ and Fe3+ in environment and human body. At present, the common methods for detecting these two ions are atomic absorption spectrometry [5], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [6], and electrochemical method [7]. Although the methods possess distinguished sensitivity and simultaneous multielement analysis, they are quite complex, costly and time consuming process. To overcome these shortcomings, various sensor platform systems have been developed, among which the fluorescent probes-based organic molecules [8], metal nanoparticles [9] and semiconductor quantum dots [10] have received extensive attention because of high selectivity, sensitivity, fast analysis and simplicity.

Carbon dots have been attracted extensive attention as a new type of zero-dimensional carbon material in the past fifteen years [11]. Compared with organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots and metal nanoclusters, carbon dots have some unique advantages, such as good aqueous solubility, resistance to photobleaching, excellent solubility, good biosafety, multiple functional groups and a broader photoluminescence spectrum, and thus have been applied in sensing, bioimaging and medical diagnosis [12–14]. With regard to the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+, a few of the fluorescence probes based carbon dots have been developed, however, most fluorescent carbon quantum dots require long-term dialysis treatment or complicated operation during the preparation process. For instance, biochar dots made from waste biomass by hydrothermal method needed to be purified with overnight dialysis and be freeze dried for 48–72 h [15]. The N, S co-doped graphene quantum dots reported by Yang et al had to be purified via ten-hour dialysis [16]. Liu et al developed a novel fluorescence sensor based carbon nanodots by hydrothermal treatment of melamine and citric acid followed by two-day dialysis [17]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop asimple and lesstime-consuming preparation method of highly sensitive sensors based-carbon dots for the determination of Hg2+ and Fe3+.

Carbon dots based solid-state luminescent materials have important application in light-emitting devices owing to their outstanding optical properties. At present, most carbon dots can emitfluorescence only in the solution. This is because fluorescence quenching of carbon dots often happens in the solid state because of their aggregation. To gain the solid state fluorescence of carbon dots, the common method is to imbed carbon dots into solid matrices such as starch, silica xerogel, inorganic salts, polymers and mesoporous materials [18,19]. However, the solid state fluorescence of carbon dots without solid matrix has been developed recently. Xia et al reported a method to prepare N, S-doped graphene quantum dots with yellow-green solid-state fluorescence from citric acid and L-cysteine. The cross-linking among L-cysteine molecules and graphene nucleus prevent the aggregation of grapheme quantum dots [20]. Chen reported a way to prepare carbon nanomaterials with strong yellow-green solid-state fluorescence with poly (vinyl alcohol) (PVA) and ethylenediamine. The PVA chains could prevent the direct contact of graphitizing cores with each other [21]. Since the reported kinds of solid-state fluorescent carbon dots are limited, it is still necessary to develop new solid-state fluorescent carbon dots to meet different practical application.

This work aims to develop asimple and lesstime-consuming preparation method of carbon dots-based sensors for the determination of Hg2+ and Fe3+ by using tartaric acid and L-arginine as precursor, and to investigate the fluorescence property in aqueous solution and solid-state. Interestingly, the nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) show blue fluorescence in aqueous solution and yellow-green fluorescence in solid state. Finally, the detection method of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in real water samples was established by using NCQDs.

All chemical reagents were analytical grade and without purification. L-arginine was purchased from Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Tartaric acid was purchased from Xi'an Chemical Reagent Factory (Shaanxi, China). Quinine sulfate dihydrate was obtained from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Standard stock solutions (4 mM) of metal ions (Ag+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cr3+) were prepared with ultrapure water from the respective metal salts (AgNO3, Cd(NO3)2, CoCl2·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, FeCl3, HgCl2, MgSO4, MnCl2, NaCl, NiCl2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(CH3COO)2, Al(NO3)3.

The morphology and size were observed on a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, H-600, FEI Electron optics). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a TENSOR27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with PHI5000 VersaProbe III (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). The fluorescence spectra were measured with a fluorescence spectrometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan). Absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were performed on a photon-counting spectrometer (FLSP920, Edinburgh Instruments, England) with λex = 370 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The NCQDs were prepared by solvothermal method with tartaric acid and L-arginine as carbon and nitrogen sources. In brief, 0.1 g tartaric acid and 0.87 g L-arginine were dissolved in 10 mL ethanol. The mixture was transferred into a 15 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (KH-15ML, Xi’an, China) and heated at 180 ℃ for 12 h in an oven. The autoclave was cooled to room temperature and the dark grown solution was obtained. Subsequently, the solution was purified by silica column using petroleum and ether-ethyl acetate as eluents. After evaporating the solvent and drying the residue under vacuum, the yellow solids were obtained.

The quantum yield (QY) of NCQDs was calculated by comparing that of the reference, quinine sulfate (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The calculation formula is as follows:
Q c
=
Q s
×
I c
×
A s
×
η
c
2
I s
×
A c
×
η
s
2
where Q is the quantum yield, I is the integrated emission intensity, A is the ultraviolet absorption intensity at 350 nm, η is the refractive index of the solvent. The subscript “s” and “c” refer to quinine sulfate and NCQDs, respectively.

100 μL of Hg2+ or Fe3+ solutions were added into 1.9 mL of 25 μg/mL NCQDs phosphate buffer solution (10 mM) with different pH values. After 2 min later, the fluorescence intensity of resulting solutions was measured with excitation wavelength at 350 nm; both excitation and emission slit widths were 5 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of Hg2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: 1.9 mL of NCQDs solutions (25 μg/mL) in phosphate buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.0) were mixed with 0.1 mL of Hg2+ or Fe3+ at different concentrations. After incubating at room temperature for 2 min, the fluorescence intensities of mixed solutions were measured with excitation at 350 nm. The fluorescence intensities were recorded at room temperature.

In the selectivity experiment, the NCQDs solutions were mixed with the metal ions of Cr3+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Na+ and Ag+, respectively. The concentrations of metal ion were 200 μM. In the interference experiment, the concentration of interference ions was 8 times that of Hg2+ (50 μM) and 2.5 times that of Fe3+ (100 μM).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detecting of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water and river water were collected from our lab and the Moat (Xi’an, China), respectively. Water samples were centrifuged at 13,200 g for 20 min and then were filtered through 0.22 μm membrane. Then, 1.9 mL of NCQDs solutions (25 μg/mL) in phosphate buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.0) were mixed with 0.1 mL tap water or river. The fluorescence intensity at λem = 425 nm was measured. The recovery experiments were carried out by spiking the tap water and river with Hg2+ or Fe3+ at three levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and chemical constitution of NCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To date, most of the preparation procedures of carbon dots require time-consuming dialysis purification operation. In contrast, the purification of NCQDs was performed by column chromatography, shortening the purification time.

From the TEM of NCQDs in Fig. S1, it is observed that the NCQDs are well monodispersed and uniform with average diameter of 1.3 nm in the narrow range of 1.1–2.0 nm. Fig. S2 shows the chemical constitution of the NCQDs. The absorption peaks are attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH (3342 cm−1) [22], CH (2946 cm−1 and 2847 cm−1), CO (1661 cm−1), C-N (1415 cm−1), CC (1106 cm−1) [23] and CH of aromatic benzene (760 cm−1 and 662 cm−1) [24], respectively. XPS survey spectrum further illustrates the surface chemical constitution of the NCQDs (Fig. 1 a). There are three strong peaks at 531.0, 399.0, 284.0 eV, which are attributed to O1s, N1s, C1s [25]. The contents of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen are calculated to be 70.54%, 13.67% and 15.79%, respectively. To obtain more detail information about the surface composition of NCQDs, the peak components of three elements in the XPS spectra were further analyzed by the core-level spectrum. As shown in Fig. 1b, the C1s spectrum shows five peaks at 283.6, 284.6, 285.8, 287.3 and 288.3 eV, which are assigned to CC/CC, CN/CO, CN, CO, and OCO groups [26], respectively. In Fig. 1c, O1s spectrum contains the two peaks at 531.0 and 531.9 eV corresponding to CO and CO, respectively [27]. The high-resolution spectrum of N1s shows four peaks at 397.7, 398.6, 399.4 and 400.2 eV (Fig. 1d), which are attributed to N in a graphite-like structure, pyridinic-like N, pyrrolic-like N, and NH groups, respectively [28]. These results suggest that NCQDs have lots of functional groups on the surface which can provide lone pair electrons for the coordination of metal ions.

The optical properties of the NCQDs were investigated in details. As seen in Fig. 2 a, the emission wavelength of NCQDs is 425 nm (red line) with excitation wavelength of 350 nm (black line). The photographic images of NCQDs (inset in Fig. 2a) show faint yellow under daylight and blue fluorescence under 365 nm. Besides, the emission wavelength of NCQDs is red-shifted with the excitation increasing from 350 nm to 400 nm (Fig. 2b), which is also displayed clearly in the corresponding normalized spectra (Fig. 2c). The fluorescence emission of excitation-dependent is attributed to the size effect and surface defects [29]. By using quinine sulfate as a reference, the absolute QY of NCQDs was calculated to be 8.3%. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetime of NCQDs was measured to be 6.61 ns at 425 nm with the excitation wavelength of 370 nm (Fig. 2d).

More interestingly, the solid NCQDs show luminous performance (Fig. S3). At present, most carbon dots emit fluorescence only in solutions because carbon dots always undergo fluorescence quenching in the solid state due to excessive resonance energy transfer or π-π interactions [18]. In this work, the NCQDs can show strongly visible yellow-green fluorescence in their solid state without solid matrices under UV lamp (Fig. S3 a and b). Therefore, the fluorescence of NCQDs has different colors in solution and solid state. There are two main reasons for this phenomenon. One reason is that the gap between excited state and ground state of predominant localized state may be narrower than that of intrinsic state, resulting in red-shift of fluorescence emission. Secondly, the regular arrangement of monomers in solid state results in the expansion of π-π conjugated area so that the energy band of solid emission becomes narrower, which brings on the yellow luminescence of solid carbon dots [30,31]. The unique fluorescence characteristics provide the possibility for the application of NCQDs in optoelectronic devices and other fields in which solid fluorescent agents are required. Herein, NCQDs are used as fluorescence ink, which is popular for patterns printing or anti-counterfeit application. Because the concentration of NCQDs solutions is very low (0.5 mg/L to 4 mg/L), the letters are invisible under daylight (Fig. S3c), but handwriting on a filter paper can be clearly observed under UV irradiation (Fig. S3d). This result suggests that NCQDs can be used for loading important information for secret communications. Moreover, the invisible fluorescent ink made by NCQDs is pollution free, and easily washable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: For practical sensing application, the fluorescence material must be stable to external environment. Fig. S4 shows the effects of external illumination time, ionic strength and pH on the stability of NCQDs. The fluorescence intensity of carbon dots remains unchanged even after continuous excitation with a 150 W Xe lamp for 60 min (Fig. S4a), in the concentration range of NaCl from 0.0 to 2.0 M (Fig. S4b) and pH 3 to 12 (Fig. S4c), respectively. The stable fluorescence intensities could be understood in terms of the change in surface charge owing to protonation-deprotonation [32]. This result indicates that the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs shows resistance to high salt concentration, suggesting that NCQDs have good photostability and chemical stability.

The NCQDs were applied in the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+. To evaluate the selectivity of this sensing system towards Hg2+ and Fe3+, the fluorescence intensities of NCQDs solutions were analyzed in the presence of various metal ions including Cd2+, Cr3+, Hg2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Na+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Zn2+ and Ag+ with the same concentration of 200 μM. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence intensities of NCQDs significantly decrease with the addition of Hg2+ and Fe3+ whereas other metal ions have no apparent quenching effect of the fluorescence. Fig. S5 displays the results of interference tests. The fluorescence intensities of NCQDs have no apparently change when Hg2+ or Fe3+ is mixed with the interference metal ions. Thus, NCQDs could be used to detect Hg2+ and Fe3+ in water.

To determine Hg2+ and Fe3+ effectively, other conditions including reaction time and pH were optimized. As shown in Fig. S6, the reactions complete within 2 and 1 min, respectively. Such a quick response suggests that NCQDs can be applied to the real-time detecting of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in the real samples. The pH of solution often has great effects on the interaction between NCQDs and metal ions. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of NCQDs gradually increases in pH range from 3 to 7 and decreases in pH range of from 7 to 11 (Fig. S7a). In the acid condition, fluorescence quenching efficiency decreases due to the protonation of the surface functional groups of NCQDs such as NH2. Due to the electrostatic repulsion between the protonated NCQDs and the positive charge of the mercury ions, the NCQDs are difficult to form complexes with mercury ions. In similar situations, in alkaline solution, the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm in the presence of Hg2+ also shows lower quenching efficiency because of the hydrolysis of mercury ions. However, pH has no noticeable effects on the detection of Fe3+ (Fig. S7b). In conclusion, the optimal pH was set at 7.0 for further experiments.

In case of the coexistence of Hg2+ and Fe3+, the current detection conditions cannot distinguish Hg2+ from Fe3+. However, it was found that the addition of thiourea can distinguish Hg2+ from Fe3+. As shown in Fig. S8a, the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs solutions is quenched with the addition of Hg2+, but the addition of thiourea can enhance the fluorescence intensity. In addition, only the addition of thiourea to the NCQDs solutions has no significant effect on fluorescence intensity. Nevertheless, there is no change of the fluorescence intensity of NCQDs-Fe3+ solutions with the addition of thiourea (Fig. S8b). The reason is due to the stronger binding affinity between Hg2+ and thiourea than that of Hg2+ and NCQDs. But thiourea failed to compete with NCQDs for Fe3+ because of the thiourea is not a good chelating ligand of iron ions. Therefore, when Hg2+ and Fe3+ coexist in the detection sample, it is necessary to add thiourea into the NCQDs solution for the separate determination of Hg2+ and Fe3+.

Fig. 4 shows the dependence of fluorescence intensity quenching of NCQDs on concentration of Hg2+ and Fe3+. The fluorescence intensity of NCQDs at 425 nm gradually decreases upon increasing Hg2+ concentrations in the range of 0–200 μM (Fig. 4a). A linear relationship between fluorescence intensity and Hg2+ concentrations is obtained with R2 = 0.997 in the range of 0–5.0 μM (Fig. 4b). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.017 μM (~8.16 ppb) for Hg2+ based on a signal-to-noise ratio of S/N = 3. According to the standard set by World Health Organization, the LOD is close to the limit of Hg2+ (6 ppb) in drinking water [33]. Fig. 4c shows the fluorescence spectra of NCQDs in the presence of different Fe3+ concentrations and there is a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity at 425 nm and Fe3+ concentrations from 0 to 70 μM with R2 = 0.993 (Fig. 4d). The LOD of Fe3+ was calculated to be 0.50 μM based on a signal-to-noise ratio of S/N = 3, which is lower than the limit of Fe3+ in drinking water (5.357 µM) set by U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [34]. These results indicate that the fluorescent probe has potential application in the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+.

Based on the fact that the addition of thiourea can distinguish Hg2+ from Fe3, the separate detect method of Hg2+ and Fe3+ can be established in the coexisting solution of Hg2+ and Fe3+. Firstly, one portion of the mixture solution of Hg2+ and Fe3+ is added into the NCQDs solution containing thiourea; the fluorescence intensity (F1) is measured (Fig. S9) and the content of Fe3+ is calculated according to the standard curve (F ~ C). Then, another portion of the mixture solution is added into the NCQDs solution and the fluorescence intensity (F2) is obtained. Considering the additivity of fluorescence [35], the content of Hg2+ can be calculated through the linear plotting of the fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F1-F2)/F1) against concentration of Hg2+. For instance, 0.1 mL of the mixture solution of Hg2+ and Fe3+ was added into the NCQDs solution contained 200 μM thiourea and the final concentrations of Hg2+ and Fe3+ are 2.5 μM and 50 μM, respectively. Consequently, the concentration of Fe3+ is calculated to be 46.42 μM by the standard curve (F ~ C) and the concentration of Hg2+ is 2.67 μM using the fluorescence quenching efficiency in Table S1 according to the linear plotting of the fluorescence quenching efficiency against the concentration of Hg2+. The determined values are close to the real concentration of Hg2+ and Fe3+, verifying the feasibility of the method.

To reflect the practicability and superiority of the fluorescence probe, the recently reported fluorescence probes are compared with the NCQDs probe as far as preparation process, LOD, linear range and response time are concerned (Table 1 ). Although the NCQDs probe in this work presents comparable linear range and LOD, the preparation of the fluorescence probe does not need the long-time dialysis. Most importantly, the response time of the fluorescence probe (Hg2+: 2 min; Fe3+: 1 min) is rather shorter compared with the reported probes, so it can shorten the analysis time and act as a promising probe for the real-time detecting of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in the real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of response to Hg2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para:
In general, there are static and dynamic mechanisms during fluorescence quenching. The possible quenching mechanism can be explained by the Stern-volmer equation. The Stern-volmer equation is as follows:
F 0
F
- 1 =
K
s v
C
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the NCQDs at 445 nm in the absence and presence of Hg2+ and Fe3+; Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, C is the concentration of Hg2+ and Fe3+. As illustrated in Fig. S10 a and b, the function of (F0-F)/F0 and the concentrations of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in the range of 0–5.0 and 0–70 μM fit to the linear Stern-Volmer, applying that static quenching occurs in this sensing system; the Stern-Volmer quenching constants of Hg2+ and Fe3+ were 1.8 × 104 M−1 and 2.7 × 105 M−1 for Hg2+ and Fe3+, respectively. To further confirm the quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime of the NCQDs with or without Hg2+ and Fe3+ was measured. The fluorescence lifetime of NCQDs solutions varies from 6.61 ns to 7.02 ns and 6.61 ns to 6.59 ns after addition of Hg2+and Fe3+, respectively, showing no obvious change (Fig. S11). Furthermore, the zeta potential of NCQDs solutions is −36.3 mV, the values of the mixture solution turned to −0.9 mV and 1.1 mV after adding Hg2+ and Fe3+ (Fig. S12), illustrating that Hg2+ and Fe3+ coordinate with oxygen-riched and amino groups on the surface of NCQDs. These results further prove that the fluorescence quenching provoked by Hg2+ or Fe3+ is static quenching.

The fluorescence probe based NCQDs for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ was applied to the tap water and river water. The real samples were analyzed by the standard addition method (Table S2). The recoveries of Hg2+ and Fe3+ are all in the range of 86.86–112.80 % and the RSD is lower than 7.10%. Also, the measured values have no significant difference from the standard spectrophotometry for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ set by the Standard examination methods for drinking water-Metal parameters (GB/T 5750.6-2006) [45]. The results suggest that the NCQDs can be used an alternative fluorescence probe for the detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in practical applications.

In addition, tap water sample was added with the different concentrations of Hg2+ and Fe3+, and the two ions was separately determined by the method presented above (Table 2 ). The recoveries of Hg2+ and Fe3+ are in the range of 86.50–115.05% with RSD less than 5.99%. These results indicate that this method can achieve the determination of two metal ions in the mixture solution.

In summary, we synthesized the NCQDs by solvothermal method with tartaric acid and L-arginine as precursors. The prepared NCQDs are stable against pH, ionic strength and light illumination, and the quantum yield is up to 8.3%. The NCQDs have a blue fluorescence in solutions, which could act as a sensor for the high selectively detecting of Hg2+ and Fe3+. Interestingly, NCQDs appear a yellow-green fluorescence in solid state, suggesting it has potential in optoelectronic device. The NCQDs have shown the application as ink in information encryption. This fluorescent probe has been successfully used in the determination of Hg2+ and Fe3+ in tap water and river water samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775121 and 21974106) and the Scientific Research Program Funded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (No. 17JS134).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105142.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tools and tactics for the optical detection of mercuric ion

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.M. Nolan, S.J. Lippard (2008) Tools and Tactics for the Optical Detection of Mercuric Ion, Chem. Rev. 108(9): 3443-3480.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On-site quantitative Hg2+ measurements based on selective and sensitive fluorescence biosensor and miniaturized smartphone fluorescence microscope

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.K. Shan, B. Wang, H.C. Huang, D. Jiang, X.P. Wu, L. Xue, S.Y. Wang, F. Liu, On-site quantitative Hg2+ measurements based on selective and sensitive fluorescence biosensor and miniaturized smartphone fluorescence microscope, Biosens. Bioelectron. 132 (2019) 238-247.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly Fe3+-selective fluorescent nanoprobe based on ultrabright N/P co-doped carbon dots and its applicationin biological samples
